findings.description: Direct contradiction on execution topology: Overview states all pipeline stages run in parallel while Stage 2 requires Stage 2 to complete before Stage 3 begins. The document cannot be both at once.
findings.evidence: Overview: 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput.' Stage 2: 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.'
findings.suggestion: Resolve the contradiction — either update the Overview to reflect the actual sequential dependency between Stage 2 and Stage 3, or correct Stage 2 if true parallelism is intended.

findings.description: Dangling reference to non-existent Section 6 for security documentation. The document ends at Conclusions; no Section 6 exists.
findings.evidence: 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6.' Document ends at Conclusions with no Section 6 present.
findings.suggestion: Either add a Section 6 covering security considerations, inline the security content where referenced, or remove the reference.

findings.description: Batch/streaming performance claim is factually inverted. Streaming outperforms batch for small files because batch incurs setup overhead that yields no advantage at small sizes.
findings.evidence: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB.' Standard data engineering: streaming wins at small sizes due to batch setup overhead.
findings.suggestion: Correct the claim to reflect accurate performance characteristics, or provide a specific methodology and conditions that would justify the batch-faster claim.

findings.description: 99.9% latency reduction is an extraordinary claim with no supporting baseline, conditions, or methodology. As stated it is not credible.
findings.evidence: 'The in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes.' No baseline, conditions, or methodology provided.
findings.suggestion: Provide the measurement baseline and conditions, or replace with a defensible figure supported by the benchmarks cited.

findings.description: Benchmark claim is entirely unattributed — 'Benchmarks show' with no source, methodology, conditions, or date.
findings.evidence: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB.' No source named.
findings.suggestion: Attribute the benchmark: name the tool, version, conditions, and when it was run. If internal, state that and provide a reference.

findings.description: Pipeline and workflow terminology drift throughout — the document uses the terms interchangeably in some places and as distinct entities in others, without defining either.
findings.evidence: Operational Notes: 'The pipeline and workflow are monitored.' Conclusions: 'a real-time streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline.' No definitions provided.
findings.suggestion: Define pipeline and workflow as distinct terms (if they are distinct) or standardize on one term throughout.

findings.description: 'Standard retry policy' is referenced but never defined — no retry count, backoff strategy, or timeout specified.
findings.evidence: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' No definition of retry count, backoff, or timeout anywhere in document.
findings.suggestion: Define the standard retry policy inline or in a referenced appendix: maximum retries, backoff algorithm (fixed/exponential), and timeout.

findings.description: Scope contradiction: Conclusions frames real-time streaming as a future enhancement, but Stage 2 already describes the system as handling both batch and streaming processing modes.
findings.evidence: 'Future enhancements include adding a real-time streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline.' Stage 2 already discusses both batch and streaming processing modes.
findings.suggestion: Align scope: if streaming is already implemented, remove it from future enhancements. If streaming is genuinely future, remove or qualify Stage 2's streaming references.

findings.description: The 5-minute monitoring poll interval contradicts the sub-second latency claim. A system polling health endpoints every 5 minutes cannot claim sub-second end-to-end latency as a meaningful operational guarantee.
findings.evidence: Stage 4: 'The monitoring system polls health endpoints every 5 minutes.' Stage 3: 'reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9%' (implying sub-second delivery).
findings.suggestion: Clarify the scope of each claim — processing latency vs. monitoring cadence are different concerns, but the document conflates them. Explicitly scope the latency claim to data delivery, not operational observability.

findings.description: 'Large files' are handled via a separate overflow queue, but no size threshold is defined, making the behavior opaque.
findings.evidence: 'Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' No size threshold specified.
findings.suggestion: Define the size threshold that triggers the overflow queue path (e.g., files > 1GB).

findings.description: Authentication mechanism unspecified — the type (API key, OAuth, mTLS, etc.) is not named.
findings.evidence: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' No type specified.

findings.description: ETL acronym not expanded on first use.
findings.evidence: 'an internal ETL system' — ETL not expanded.
findings.suggestion: Expand to 'Extract, Transform, Load (ETL)' on first use.

findings.description: Prometheus dashboard mentioned in both Stage 4 and Operational Notes with near-identical phrasing — redundant.
findings.evidence: Stage 4: 'The monitoring system uses a Prometheus dashboard.' Operational Notes bullet 1: 'Monitored via the Prometheus dashboard.' Near-duplicate.
findings.suggestion: State it once in Stage 4 and reference it from Operational Notes, or consolidate.
